title: Deuteronomy 28
subtitle: The Great Blessings and the Great Curses: Covenant at Its Starkest

sections.header: Verses 1–44 — The Covenant Blessings; the Opening Curses

heb.gloss: if you fully obey the LORD your God
heb.paragraph: wehayah im-shamo'a tishma'a (והיה אם־שמוע תשמע) = if you fully obey the LORD your God

heb.gloss: you will be the head and not the tail
heb.paragraph: wehayita lero'sh velo lezanav (והיית לראש ולא לזנב) = you will be the head and not the tail

refs.note: The Leviticus 26 blessings and curses provide the legal-covenantal framework that Deuteronomy 28 expands dramatically. Both texts anticipate the exile and both provide the theology of return.
The fall of Israel (722 BC) and Judah (586 BC) are interpreted through the lens of Deuteronomy 28 -- the curses came upon them because they violated the covenant.

notes.note: MacArthur: The six blessing formulas ("blessed in the city... blessed in the country... blessed when you come in... blessed when you go out") are comprehensive principles: every sphere of life flourishes under covenant faithfulness. The blessings are the natural fruit of a society ordered according to God's design. Creation works as it was meant to when covenant is kept.

MacArthur: The military, agricultural, and national blessings are not magic rewards for religious compliance but the described outcomes of covenant faithfulness. "The LORD will grant you abundant prosperity -- in the fruit of your womb, the young of your livestock and the crops of your ground." These are the material expressions of the blessing of living in alignment with the Creator's design.

MacArthur: The curse formulas mirror the blessings exactly: "cursed in the city... cursed in the country... cursed when you come in... cursed when you go out." The exact reversal of blessing is not poetic symmetry but theological precision: covenant betrayal produces the opposite of what covenant faithfulness produces.

MacArthur: The progressive curses culminate in exile -- "the LORD will drive you and the king you set over you to a nation unknown to you or your ancestors." The threat of exile is not a later addition but is built into the covenant structure from the beginning. The covenant that gave the land can take it away.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The extensive curse section is not designed to terrify Israel into compliance but to make fully explicit what covenant failure means. The curses are predictive, not vindictive -- they describe what happens when a society organised around the living God turns away from him. Deuteronomy 28 functions as a covenant warning, not a threat.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: On covenant blessings; opening curses: the JPS commentary observes that this legislation reflects the distinctive Deuteronomic concern for centralisation and humanitarian ethics. Where earlier law codes state the rule, Deuteronomy adds motivation clauses that ground the law in Israel's experience of God.

Tigay: Comparative ANE evidence illuminates the background — similar provisions appear in the Code of Hammurabi and the Middle Assyrian Laws, but Deuteronomy's theological framing is unique: the law serves the covenant relationship, not merely social order.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section reflects the characteristic Deuteronomic style — parenetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

NET Note: Several terms in this passage carry technical covenant vocabulary. The repeated use ofšāmar("to keep, guard") frames obedience as vigilant custody of God's commands, not passive acquiescence.

Calvin: On covenant blessings; opening curses: the law here is not mere legislation but divine pedagogy — God shapes his people's character through specific commands that address the conditions they will face in Canaan. Every statute reflects both God's holiness and his care for human wellbeing.

Calvin: The application extends to the church in every age. What Moses commands Israel in historical particularity, the Spirit applies to believers in principle. The form changes; the underlying demand for covenant faithfulness does not.

hist.context: Chapter 28 is Deuteronomy's most dramatic chapter -- 68 verses, 14 of blessings and 54 of curses. The asymmetry is deliberate: the curses are longer and more terrifying than the blessings because the stakes of covenant failure are catastrophic. The blessings are agricultural, domestic, military, and national: fruitful fields, blessed cities, victory over enemies, rain in season, Israel as head among nations. The curses progressively intensify through the first half of the chapter before reaching their most extreme forms in vv.45-68.

sections.header: Verses 45–68 — The Great Curses: Siege, Exile, Return to Egypt

heb.gloss: because you did not serve the LORD your God
heb.paragraph: tachat asher lo-'avadeta et-YHWH (תחת אשר לא־עבדת את־יהוה) = because you did not serve the LORD your God

The siege language of vv.52-57 reaches horrifying specificity: the siege will be so severe that parents eat their own children and hide food from family members. The description is not hyperbole but accurate prophecy of what siege warfare does to a starving population.

refs.note: The four horsemen of the Apocalypse -- war, famine, plague, death -- recapitulate the Deuteronomy 28 curse sequence in eschatological form.

"With their own hands compassionate women have cooked their own children, who became their food when my people were destroyed." Lamentations documents the fulfilment of Deuteronomy 28:57's most horrifying curse during the siege of Jerusalem.

notes.note: MacArthur: "Because you did not serve the LORD your God joyfully and gladly in the time of prosperity, therefore in hunger and thirst, in nakedness and dire poverty, you will serve the enemies the LORD sends against you." The most searching verse in the chapter: the root cause of Israel's future disaster is not dramatic apostasy but the failure to serve God joyfully. Grudging, joyless, mechanical religious compliance is already the beginning of covenant failure.

MacArthur: "If you do not carefully follow all the words of this law... the LORD will send fearful plagues on you and your descendants, harsh and prolonged disasters, and severe and lingering illnesses." The progression from disobedience to disaster is gradual -- the curses escalate through stages, not all at once. God's judgment is patient, not impulsive.

MacArthur: The exile curse -- return to Egypt in ships, sold as slaves, no buyers -- is the most extreme reversal of the Exodus. Israel began in Egypt as slaves; they will return to Egypt as slaves. History moves in covenant circles: what God did in grace, covenant failure can undo. The anti-Exodus is the covenant's most terrifying threat.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: The threat of exile "to a land unknown to you or your ancestors" was remarkably fulfilled in the Assyrian and Babylonian exiles. The specificity of the Deuteronomic curses has led many scholars to date Deuteronomy as post-exilic prophecy after the fact. The Kline/Craigie position is that the specificity reflects genuine prophetic foresight within the Mosaic covenant structure.

Tigay: The JPS commentary highlights that this section's legislation has parallels in other ANE legal collections but is distinctively reframed within the Deuteronomic theology of covenant love and centralised worship.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: On the great curses: siege and exile: the structure here follows the pattern of ANE treaty stipulations — specific obligations laid on the vassal by the suzerain. The parallels with Hittite treaty forms confirm that Deuteronomy's legal material is presented in a recognised international diplomatic format.

Craigie: The literary placement of this section within the larger address serves a rhetorical function — Moses builds from general principles to specific applications, a technique well attested in ancient Near Eastern legal and wisdom literature.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 68 — The Great Curses: Siege, Exile, Return to Egypt: enetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

Calvin: On 68 — The Great Curses: Siege, Exile, Return to Egypt: ngle human leader can bear the weight of God's people alone — not Moses, not any pastor or bishop. Moses' delegation is wise governance and theological humility simultaneously. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

hist.context: The second half of the curse section intensifies dramatically: disease, defeat, madness, exile, siege so severe parents eat their children, return to Egypt as slaves with no buyers. Many scholars note that the curse section closely parallels the Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC) -- either as shared ANE treaty formula or as evidence of a specific relationship. The curses are not designed to be fulfilled but to be avoided -- yet the historical narrative of Joshua through Kings shows them progressively fulfilled.

ppl.name: Joshua↗ People
ppl.role: Moses’s military commander and designated successor
ppl.text: Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.

ppl.name: Og↗ People
ppl.role: Giant king of Bashan; last of the Rephaim
ppl.text: Og king of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim — a pre-Israelite giant people. His iron bed, measuring nine cubits long (c.4.5m), was preserved in Rabbah of Ammon as a monument (Deut 3:11). Israel defeated him at Edrei after his defeat of Sihon, and Moses divided his territory among the Transjordan tribes. Deut 3 presents his defeat as proof that God can give Israel the whole land: if the giant Og fell, so will the Anakites in Canaan. He appears in numerous later biblical summaries of God’s power (Ps 135, 136; Neh 9).

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (14th–13th c. BC)
src.quote: Klaus Baltzer (1960) and Meredith Kline (1963) demonstrated that Deuteronomy follows the six-part structure of Hittite vassal treaties: preamble (1:1–5), historical prologue (1:6–3:29), stipulations (4–26), document clause (27:1–9), witnesses (31:19–22), and blessings/curses (28). This structure is attested only in 14th–13th century BC treaties, strongly supporting Mosaic authorship.
src.note: The treaty-form evidence is the most powerful external argument for Deuteronomy’s early date. Subsequent Assyrian vassal treaties (7th c. BC) have a different form, making a late (Josian) dating of the book structurally improbable.

src.title: Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC)
src.quote: The Neo-Assyrian VTE contains curse language strikingly similar to Deut 28, leading some scholars (Weinfeld, Steymans) to argue Deuteronomy was modelled on Assyrian treaties. However, the VTE represents only the curse section; Deuteronomy’s full six-part structure matches only Hittite treaties.
src.note: The parallel demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern covenant forms were widespread, but the direction of dependence is disputed. The Hittite parallel is structurally closer and temporally earlier, supporting the traditional dating.

src.title: Ugaritic and Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Terminology
src.quote: The vocabulary of Deuteronomy —berît(covenant),ahavah(love as covenant loyalty),hesed(loyal love), ‘adon’ (lord) — is attested in Ugaritic and other ANE diplomatic texts as standard covenant terminology.
src.note: This shows that Deuteronomy’s language of love, loyalty, and service was not merely personal piety but carried legal-diplomatic weight: Israel’s relationship with God is structured as a formal suzerainty covenant.

rec.who: Origen, Homilies on Deuteronomy (3rd c. AD)
rec.text: "Origen read Deuteronomy christologically throughout, interpreting Moses’ farewell discourses as prefiguring Christ’s teaching, the “prophet like Moses” (18:15) as a direct prophecy of Christ, and the land as a figure of the soul’s heavenly inheritance."

"MacArthur: The six blessing formulas ("blessed in the city... blessed in the country... blessed when you come in... blessed when you go out") are comprehensive principles: every sphere of life flouris…"

hebtext.gloss: if you fully obey the LORD your God
hebtext.note: The double verb (shama + tishma) is the Hebrew intensive: not merely "if you obey" but "if you truly, fully, faithfully obey." The blessings are conditional on complete covenant faithfulness. The standard is not perfectionism (the sacrificial system provides for failure) but wholehearted orientation.

hebtext.gloss: you will be the head and not the tail
hebtext.note: The head/tail metaphor (v.44) summarises the reversal that covenant failure brings: Israel, called to be a leader among nations, becomes a follower; called to lead, becomes enslaved. The image reverses the election blessing of ch.7 with exact precision.

hebtext.gloss: because you did not serve the LORD your God
hebtext.note: The root cause of Israel's future disaster is stated explicitly in v.47: not dramatic apostasy but the failure to serve God "joyfully and gladly in the time of prosperity." Grudging, joyless, mechanical religious compliance is already the beginning of covenant failure.

The siege language of vv.52-57 reaches horrifying specificity: the siege will be so severe that parents eat their own children and hide food from family members. The description is not hyperbole but accurate prophecy of what siege warfare does to a starving population.

tx.title: LXX Deuteronomy and the MT divergences
tx.content: MTandLXXDeuteronomy diverge more significantly than other Pentateuchal books. The LXX adds explanatory expansions, especially in the blessings and curses (ch.28) and the Song of Moses (ch.32).
tx.note: The LXX Deuteronomy was translated with greater theological freedom than other books. It often interprets rather than translates, and has shaped the NT’s use of Deuteronomy (Paul cites LXX Deut in Rom 10 and Gal 3).

tx.title: Qumran and the Samaritan Pentateuch
tx.content: Deuteronomy is the most-copied book at Qumran (29 manuscripts), showing its centrality to Second Temple Judaism. TheSPDeuteronomy adds a tenth commandment mandating an altar on Gerizim (Exod 20:17b insert), the central SP polemic.
tx.note: The Nash Papyrus (c.150–100 BC) preserves the Decalogue and Shema from Deuteronomy — the oldest surviving Hebrew manuscript of any biblical text. It shows a mixed text-type combining MT and LXX features.

positions.name: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.proponents: The dominant critical view since de Wette (1805) identifies the “book of the law” found in 2 Kgs 22 with Deuteronomy, placing its composition in Josiah’s reign (c.621 BC). Deuteronomy on this view was written to legitimise Josiah’s centralisation reforms.
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.name: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.proponents: The Hittite suzerainty treaty structure (attested only in 14th–13th c. BC) strongly supports early composition. The book’s internal claims (1:1, 31:9,22,24) and NT citations (Jesus cites Deuteronomy three times in the temptation: Matt 4:4,7,10) support Mosaic origin.
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

themes.note: The Great Blessings and the Great Curses: Covenant at Its Starkest

coaching.tip: Blessings cascaded: in city and country, womb and crop, basket and kneading trough (vv. 3-6). Obedience brought flourishing in every domain. The vision was total prosperity—nothing exempted from covenant benefit.

The blessing catalog (vv. 1-14) described a nation so prosperous that other nations would recognize God's favor: 'all peoples on earth will see that you are called by the name of the LORD' (v. 10). Israel's obedience was meant to be visible testimony — the same missional logic Paul applied to the church: 'so that the body of Christ may be built up' (Eph 4:12). Covenant faithfulness was never private; it was always meant to provoke recognition.

Curses intensified through stages: disease, drought, defeat, madness, exile (vv. 15-68). The catalog grew horrific—siege cannibalism, scattering among nations. Disobedience dismantled everything blessing built. The stakes were total.

The curse section (vv. 15-68) is four times longer than the blessing section (vv. 1-14). The asymmetry is deliberate — Deuteronomy spent more words on warning than on promise. The curses escalated from agricultural failure to military defeat to siege cannibalism to exile and psychological terror. The final curse reversed the exodus itself: 'the LORD will send you back in ships to Egypt' (v. 68). History undone, liberation cancelled, the story run backwards.

prayer_prompt: Blessings for obedience filled the land; curses for disobedience filled seventy verses. Consider asking God which path you're on — and whether there's time to change direction.
